DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Oct-24-2019 Overruled
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Annie Wu, Esq. 415-673-4800 of the Veen Firm for Plaintiff.
Alexander Davis, Esq. 650-856-2400 of Baker McKenzie for Defendant VELOFIX HOLDINGS USA.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court shares its views. Defendant argues. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT VELOFIX HOLDINGS USA, INC.'S DEMURRER TO PLAINTIFFS RACHAEL GOLDBLATT AND BRAD GOLDBLATT'S 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT PURSUANT TO CODE CIV. PROC. 430.10.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Defendant Velofix Holdings USA, Inc.'s demurrer to the amended complaint is overruled in its entirety. Plaintiff alleges sufficient facts showing that defendant could be liable based on an agency relationship with Velofix Bay Area, which is alleged to have distributed or sold the bicycle. (Amended Complaint, 6-10, 17; see Arriaga v. CitiCapital Commercial Corp. (2008) 167 Cal.App.4th 1527, 1534 ["Beyond manufacturers, anyone identifiable as 'an integral part of the overall producing and marketing enterprise' is subject to strict liability"]; Bay Summit Community Assn. v. Shell Oil Co. (1996) 51 Cal.App.4th 762, 772-773 [parties subject to strict liability include manufacturers, retailers, others involved in the vertical distribution of consumer goods including wholesale and retail distributors and licensors, and nonmanufacturing parties outside the vertical chain of distribution of a product which profit from placing the product into the stream of commerce]; Cislaw v. Southerland Corp. (1992) 4 Cal.App.4th 1284, 1289 [franchise agreement may give rise to agency relationship].) In addition, the amended complaint alleges that defendant sold the bicycle at issue to the Fairmont Hotel. (Amended Complaint, 17.) Such alternative pleading is permissible. (See Adams v. Paul (1995) 11 Cal.4th 583, 593 ["a party may plead in the alternative and may make inconsistent allegations"].) Discovery will resolve the precise details of the bicycle transaction, and there remain factual issues regarding whether the Fairmont Hotel purchased the bicycle directly from defendant's website and what precise role defendant played in its distribution or sale, which could support the strict liability and negligence claims. (See Bay Summit Community Assn., 51 Cal.App.4th at 774 ["In applying this stream of commerce theory, the courts have eschewed legal labels and have taken a very practical approach, focusing on the actual connection between the defendant's activities and the defective product."].)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Sep-13-2019 Continued To Dec-04-19 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Demurrer To Complaint (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only)
Off Calendar Aug-07-2019 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES:
Demurrer To Complaint (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only) is a duplicate entry. (302)

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Aug-07-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearance.

RULING: There being no appearance or objection, the Court adopted the tentative ruling on DEFENDANT VELOFIX HOLDINGS USA, INC.'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT, as follows:

Defendant Velofix Holdings USA, Inc.'s demurrer to Plaintiffs Rachel Goldblatt and Brad Goldblatt's complaint is sustained as to all seven causes of action with 20 days leave to amend. The allegation that Velofix Holdings "partners" with mobile bike workshops such as Defendant Velofix Bay Area is insufficient to indicate where Velofix Holdings fits into the stream of commerce and Plaintiffs' theories of liability. (Compl. Paras. 4-5; see Arriaga v. CitiCapital Commercial Corp. (2008) 167 Cal.App.4th 1527, 1534 [defendant is subject to strict products liability if identifiable as an "integral part of the overall producing and marketing enterprise"] [emphasis added]; Bay Summit Community Assn. v. Shell Oil Co. (1996) 51 Cal.App.4th 762, 776 [products liability claims require "a sufficient causative relationship or connection between the defendant and the product so as to establish" that the defendant received a direct financial benefit from the sale of the product, was integrally involved in the business, and had control over or a substantial ability to influence the manufacturing or distribution process].) The complaint contains no other allegations specific to Velofix Holdings, only generic allegations against Defendants collectively. (See Hawkins v. TAC Int'l Airlines, S.A. (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 466, 478-479 [boilerplate allegations are insufficient to survive demurrer]; Doheny Park Terrace Homeowners Assn., Inc. v. Truck Ins. Exchange (2005) 132 Cal.App.4th 1076, 1099 [complaint must make more than generic allegations that fail to set forth the essential facts with sufficient particularity to acquaint each defendant with "the nature, source and extent of [each] cause of action"].) By contrast, the alleged role of each of the other defendants is made clearer. (See Compl. Paras. 5-6, 12 [alleging that Velofix Bay Area "sells, repair and services bicycles" in San Francisco; Defendant Bay Area Bike Services, LLC provides "on-site repair"; and Defendant Priority Bicycles "manufactured, distributed, and sold" the bike Ms. Goldblatt rode when she was injured].)

While pleading in the alternative is generally acceptable, Plaintiffs have not sufficiently alleged how Velofix Holdings is alleged to be connected to the bicycle enterprise and Ms. Goldblatt's injury for purposes of strict liability. Furthermore, this is not a situation involving fraudulent concealment that justifies a less specific form of pleading as was the case in Turner v. Milstein (1951) 103 Cal.App.2d 651, 658. In addition, without more clarity on the role of Velofix Holdings, the negligence claims are not properly supported as it is unclear what duty was breached. (See Minnegren v. Nozar (2016) 4 Cal.App.5th 500, 507 [negligence claims require allegations that support the conclusion that the defendant breached a duty of care to the plaintiff].)

Plaintiffs' opposition suggests that they may have additional facts they can plead regarding Velofix Holdings' role in the sale of bikes like the one that allegedly injured Ms. Goldblatt. They are granted leave to amend to add such allegations and to clarify how Velofix Holdings specifically is connected to the bicycle enterprise and how it owed a duty to Ms. Goldblatt.

The Court signed the order submitted by email.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Not reported.
